key_insight: LLM4MOF uses language-model agents to generate interpretable design hypotheses and then test them in simulation, enabling closed-loop inverse design of metal-organic frameworks without training a separate model for each target property.
engineering_relevance: The framework offers a practical way to search vast MOF design spaces for adsorption, separation, and electronic-structure tasks while keeping the design logic explainable and simulation-grounded.
method: One agent proposes hypotheses over metal nodes, linkers, pore geometry, and functional chemistry.
A second agent converts those hypotheses into constraints that filter candidate MOFs built from a metal node, organic linker, and matching topology.
Each hypothesis is evaluated with four diagnostic beams to isolate the effects of geometry, chemistry, and metal choice.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports success across six tasks within 400 property evaluations, plus de novo MOF generation that outperforms random search and a genetic algorithm at roughly $1 per campaign.
watch_item: Performance depends on simulation quality and on how well the agent-generated constraints capture valid chemistry, so errors in reasoning or surrogate assumptions could limit generalization.
paper_title: Interpretable Inverse Design of Metal-Organic Frameworks with Large Language Model Agents
